sentences.text: Nobody knew.
The refugees come from the other part of Poland.
And they come by us.
And we took in every-- every house had to take in so many refugees.
And they didn't stay too long till the-- till the war broke out between Germany and-- and-- and the Russian.
And I think it was 1940-- first of all.
In 1939, I remember I did go look up-- again, I'm foolish [INAUDIBLE].
And I-- I really thinks.
And they were one chasing each other.
And I'm just looking at them, because then the Polish people, the Polish, whatever army they had.
They didn't have an army.
They have-- they have an army running bicycles, cavalry, cavalry running.
They didn't have no, not mechanized.
They maybe had five planes, nothing.
Yeah, oh, yeah, I remember.
I think it didn't took-- I think it was altogether, I think Warsaw hold out for 15 days.
But otherwise, crumbled on the Germans.
You know, they were all mechanized.
They just blow them out of the map.
They-- they say the truth in-- in BBC.
They tell everything.
And later on, in the beginning, they didn't say too much.
I don't know why.
But later on, as the war got intensified, they used to tell about everything.
Yes.
In 1939, when the Russian come in, they-- they didn't discriminate against Jews, though.
Every day, they opened all the schools.
And anybody was willing to go to school, you could go to school.
And you could do just about as far as a lot of things, libraries and a lot of cultural things.
But I couldn't get a job because it was marked in my passport, I am a son of a businessman.
Can you believe that?
I-- and I was a young guy at that time.
And they wouldn't give me a job for that reason.
Did they take away your father's business, the Russians?
My father give them the business.
He had that brother what he had in Russia.
And he told them, he said, you know?
You want to have-- you want to have peace of mind, give him the business.
What are you going to do it?
They're going to get it anyway from you.
So he gave him the business.
And they made him in charge, you know.
But he lasted-- he there, work for a while.
And I don't know what happened.
Yeah, then this-- that it was his business.
They-- they told him he's stealing money from that.
But he didn't.
But they-- they took inventory.
And they find out there's extra money or something.
So they come to the conclusion, he was stealing money.
So then they fired him from his business.
But didn't-- they didn't pay nothing for that, though.
Nothing.
And we are as far, and we still, you know, when they talk about war, we had a special room and they stacked up it a lot of meat and all kinds of things.
And so we had enough to get by for a long time.
Yeah.
I probably would have stayed in the same business, you know?
I mean, then, you know, years of business, and there's only two-- two boys, like my father.
And he was getting older.
And he says, like he took over from his father, you will have to take over and run it from us, for you and your brother, whatever.
But I think my brother, earlier, he was older, and he could get a job in Rowne.
I wonder was he doing over there.
I think he was greasing cars or whatever, trucks.
No, no, no car.
The life was-- was same.
In 1941 when the Germans come over, that's-- then you could hear already like 1941, this is, you know, at town so-and-so, they killed 100 people, here 50 people.
But the people still wanted-- didn't want to believe it, though till it happened to them.
Then everyone-- the rabbi said that everybody has to go and pray and everything would be OK.
It didn't help, though.
So more he tell the people to pray and not to go, you know, and the most people they survived whatever it was is younger people.
And older people couldn't make it.
Life was too harsh.
Maybe there was some women, but very few.
No.
Like I told you, they didn't nobody want to believe that such a thing exist.
They thought maybe, ah, they-- it's over, going over-blowing.
It's not going to happen to them.
Otherwise, they would probably-- they had money.
They probably would think maybe to flee the Poland and go to some other country or whatever.
I don't know.
But there never was a talk like that I could hear of this.
Most-- most people in Poland left.
If they were poor, they couldn't make a living.
So they come over here in United States, or wherever, or Canada.
And so that's what I know.
Yeah.
Mm, let's see.
In 1941, and the date, I don't remember exact.
But anyway, they come in.
They were very polite.
But then the Ukrainian, they told them they were free and to do anything they want to do.
What did they do?
They-- they have another pogrom, and they killed people.
And they got into houses and took their things.
And after that, they-- they established a ghetto.
I mean, but that was a small ghetto.
Yeah.
But by that time, was nothing left.
Wasn't-- and they liked to take like furs.
And-- and if you have gold or something, they would like to have gold and furs.
Some of them neighbors, they knew you so good.
And you would think, this is the people they're going to spare you.
But they come in, and they were rough, you know?
And some people, they killed too.
Yeah.
No.
No.
But they come in, and if some people resisted them, they tell them they own everything.
And they says, they-- every-- every Jew that a gentile talked to is-- is rich.
And there was a lot of Jewish people.
There was some-- what do you call it-- tailors and just regular working people.
They didn't have no money.
They had-- that's-- they struggled to-- to make a living.
Well, I think most of them, they leave them-- they left them alone.
They only did go to business people.
If you had a business or something, they wanted.
But later on, they just in '42, they liquidated, little by little.
The ghetto, the ghetto was a very small ghetto.
And everybody got along really pretty good there in the beginning.
Yeah.
In, yeah, yeah, in the same area.
They just made an area in the same town, and most of it was the same.
We stayed in my house, but there was restrictions.
They want to have-- they want to-- first, they want to have-- they line up a bunch of people.
And they says, well, and they have orders to have so much, so much money to raise by tomorrow.
Otherwise, they're going to kill so many people.
And so they had to come up.
And once they come up, twice.
And by the end, the people didn't have the money.
So just-- they took every third or fourth person to line them up, and they just killed them.
And it means a warning.
The next time, they're going to kill all of them.
Yeah.
In the main square.
And they lined them up, not-- not children, only grown-up people.
Only men that time.
And later on, I think, in '42-- '42, yeah, was '42, then they started to liquidate it in the ghetto.
And they did it in a fashion [INAUDIBLE].
And they said the harvest, they're going to give so much pounds of wheat, of flour.
They're going to help the-- to harvest the wheat because otherwise, it's going to rot in the fields.
And so my mother, I think, she was the first one to go.
I didn't go and neither did my father.
But she did go.
And the first day, the first day, they give them wheat.
Only when it's flour they given them, each ones.
And the next-- they says, they need more people.
They can use all the people they can get.
And then when they get all them people, they just make them dig ditches, and they just the-- the Germans were-- the Germans were in charge, but the Ukrainian were using the guns.
What do you mean?
Yeah.
A-- every-- every-- every-- what do you call it?
Every ghetto had a-- a committee.
But what smaller the town they were, they were not as-- as vicious.
You know what I mean?
No.
But-- but they-- but they put a lot of-- they tell them where they want to every time from then.
Most of what is they want is they want money.
That's what they want.
It's a open, more or less one.
In this one, probably, I blocked it out of my mind.
I can't tell.
Yeah.
In [INAUDIBLE].
I remember, it was [INAUDIBLE], in [INAUDIBLE] in '42.
I think '42.
The ghetto.
Yeah, but that's when the last of the Jews.
In August, the [INAUDIBLE].
That was the last one.
My father lived till the last one.
Yeah.
Mhm.
I know.
I even have a-- I made like a little bit like a-- and I marked it down.
And-- how you say-- like a little matzevah.
And I have it at home.
And it's marked 1942, [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE].
My mother-- my mother went to help to collect the harvest.
And they paid him really a good, because there was by that time, there wasn't enough food.
And so they said the next morning, they can use more people.
And then all the-- when they had enough people, they make him dig the ditches.
And they machine gun them all.
At this time, I went to that-- what do you call it?
To the--
--to the governess.
I didn't know that that would happen, but I did go for food.
And then-- then everybody knew already that they massacred so many Jews.
And she said to me, you know what?
Maybe you shouldn't go back.
We going to help you to get to the woods.
And then my father, you know, years ago, we had a sukkah.
And I-- and I used to open up the sukkah.
And my father watch me the way I used to open the sukkah.
So he hide behind the sukkah.
And that's why he survived.
Now, you know, after they-- after when they killed all the people, they go through town and look whom they can beside, clean everybody out as much as they can.
My sister was-- my sister was hiding there in this-- under-- under the sukkah.
I was not in town after that.
And that's-- but people were there still alive.
Some of them died in Israel.
And they said to me, they seen they-- they took your sister out from there.
But they didn't know what happened.
I suppose it happened what with everybody else.
My brother's alive here.
At that time, he was too.
He run away with the other group in the-- in the woods.
I didn't know about him till the war was over.
I didn't have no contact with him.
I know when the war was over, what was it, 1945.
I mean, I didn't met him till 1946. 1946?
Yeah.
Yeah.
The last time.
Yeah.
And the last time I saw my father.
I didn't go back there no more.
With my brother.
And my brother, he was in other part of the woods.
And I didn't know.
And I didn't even know if he was alive or not.
Not too long because she told me that neighbors were telling that she's hiding some Jewish people.
And--
No, but, you know, they were telling.
And she said, and they catch me, she said, she don't mind.
But they're going to kill us all.
So why don't I take you.
And she said, there is some partisan there in their woods.
Maybe you can connect with them.
[CRYING] [SOBS] [SOBS] [DRINKS]
That's it.
Yeah, she took me to the forest.
And she told me where the other ones are hiding there.
And, you know, got by them.
What do you mean?
Yeah, she-- she was-- she-- because--
Miedzyrzecz is not far.
You know, Miedzyrzecz, Rowne.
No, no, not Rowne.
It's not Rowne.
What is it?
Hm.
What did I written down there?
I forgot.
I know I says I was in the woods there.
I seen there, not in this part.
Yeah.
You know, she told me where they are approximately.
In most of it, in the woods, there was nobody else there.
Nobody-- most of them had afraid to get in there.
If you are not, let's say, if you are Ukrainian or a Nazi or that, they tried a couple times to penetrate.
But we were very deep in the woods, and they were afraid to get in there.
There were some soldiers, were some Russian soldiers who had run away.
And there was some [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Jews.
There were-- and that's about it.
There were some Russian and Jews.
No, no.
And there is only two-- they know I was desperate because is only Jewish and-- is only Jewish and gentile.
And they know.
It's not like over here.
You can hide your identity.
They know if you're Jewish or not.
And they knew what was going on and why you're there, because you're running away.
[CRYING]
And for most of it from the same town when I am, Miedzyrzecz.
Yeah, they're in-- they're in Israel, some of them.
Spiegelman and then what are the other guy?
I think it was four or five, four or five people there.
Me and so most of it were like neighbors.
Spiegelman.
And one was Chaim, Chaim Sternsczs.
But he's not a relative.
He just another-- he lived in the same town.
But he was no relative of mine or whatever.
[CRYING]
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So I don't know.
It was very hard.
And then by the end of the war, I think, I got-- I got hit with the shrapnel.
And-- and I couldn't-- I was no use to them no more, for I mean, basically, you know?
You didn't-- I couldn't do nothing.
I had to be incapacitated in the war.
How did you get hit?
They were shooting of us because we're running away.
And they hit me right in the-- hit me right here and knocked out a couple ribs.
I think it must be the Germans because we run away.
We went after one mission and it was unsuccessful.
And they chase you all.
They chase them.
But I'm the only guy got hit.
All the other one run away.
The mission was we want to get-- get rid of a couple of Germans and take their guns.
And it didn't work, and it backfired.
In a vehicle.
You see?
I-- and see, I can't remember even that.
And this is-- this is next to Miedzyrzecz and them.
It's a forest, you know?
In Poland, there was a lot of big forest with wood.
And I couldn't even remember that.
Yeah.
We thought we were going where we could have more guns.
We could operate a better, a more efficient, you know?
And there was not, maybe 10 guns and 100 and some people.
It was not enough guns.
But, I mean, a lot of-- and then-- and then the war, I notice it.
And people got malnutrition and they got sick.
And this--
Yeah.
Yeah.
A plan is to get more guns so we can operate more efficient and-- and go out and get food.
From them, from the Germans.
They were-- they were by the railroad station.
And it's-- they are-- and they-- what do you call it?
There are certain section of the railroad station.
They were-- what do you call-- they were guarding.
And we though this was like 10 of them or 12.
And we thought that we can get only three of them, we would be OK.
But somehow, it backfired.
I don't know how.
That backfired.
They started shooting at us.
And everybody, we were on-- and you know, you couldn't-- if you don't have enough guns, and you know you're going to, so some of them took off.
I was the only guy who got hurt.
No, I don't think so.
You don't have no-- nobody has a radio or anything.
How you're going to tell them?
I mean, here you have radios.
There was no radios or any communication.
You couldn't talk on the outside world.
Yeah.
Yeah.
No.
No more home after that.
I know.
But, I mean, the same token--
I knew what my mother was killed.
And we got [INAUDIBLE] communication with it, with the town.
And they told me my father was alive.
From everybody, my father was alive.
He got killed too.
And did it.
It was two shooters.
The one was-- the one was-- like I told you, one was [INAUDIBLE] and one was [INAUDIBLE].
He was the last one.
No, my mother was [INAUDIBLE].
He was the last one.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Yeah.
We have to go out by the farmers and beg for food.
Some of them, they used to have like potatoes.
And they didn't maybe want to give it to you.
But they put on the potatoes outside.
So if something happened, they're going to tell, you know, the German catch them?
They're going to tell, he had the potatoes laid out for the-- for the animals to feed them.
And, well, in the beginning, it was very tough till we got organized.
In a bunker.
And they built a big bunker and dug up a lot of dirt.
And you were-- you know?
It wasn't bad under there.
Oh, yeah.
Are you kidding?
It just a-- a double or triple bed, whatever you could arrange it.
Wouldn't be anything.
But I don't know what they put on the top because it didn't leak, though.
The water didn't come through.
I don't know what [INAUDIBLE].
There were-- they had three of them, not all in this, in the same area.
But one didn't have a connection with the other.
Let's see, I think about 25, 30 people in each, in each bunker.
Everybody-- but there was nothing, was no-- I think they had one table, yeah, one table.
And there's no bedding, if that's what you mean.
You sleep with your clothes.
You have to sleep.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] Uh-oh.
